name: Condensed Matter Physics
description: Materials, superconductivity, semiconductors, magnetism, and quantum phases of matter. Condensed matter physics studies how large numbers of atoms and electrons collectively organize into emergent phases — solids, liquids, magnets, superconductors, topological states. It is the largest branch of physics by practitioner count and the foundation of modern electronics and computing.
axioms: Bloch's theorem: electrons in a periodic potential have wave functions of the form ψ_k(r) = e^{ik·r}u_k(r) — energy bands form
Fermi-Dirac statistics: electrons as fermions occupy states up to the Fermi energy at T=0; the Fermi surface governs transport
Phonons: quantized lattice vibrations — carry heat in insulators, mediate superconductivity, determine thermal properties
BCS theory (Bardeen-Cooper-Schrieffer): Cooper pairs form via phonon-mediated attraction below T_c → superconductivity
Landau theory of phase transitions: spontaneous symmetry breaking characterized by an order parameter
Topological phases: classified by topological invariants (Chern number, winding number), not local order parameters — robust to disorder
Anderson localization: disorder can localize electrons in a lattice — metal-insulator transition without interactions
reasoning_patterns: Band structure analysis: from the electronic band structure, determine conductor/insulator/semiconductor from Fermi surface and band gap
Quasiparticle approach: treat collective excitations as effective particles (phonons, magnons, polarons, excitons, plasmons)
Mean-field theory: approximate a many-body interacting problem by an effective single-particle field — self-consistency required
Renormalization group: systematically integrate out short-wavelength degrees of freedom to analyze critical behavior
Symmetry classification: identify the order parameter and broken symmetry of each phase (Landau paradigm)
Linear response: compute transport coefficients (conductivity, susceptibility) from correlation functions (Kubo formula)
Topological invariant computation: calculate Chern number, Z₂ invariant, or winding number to classify topological phases
boundary_conditions: High-temperature superconductivity mechanism (cuprates, iron pnictides) is still debated — no consensus theory
Quantum computing with topological qubits is frontier research — not yet experimentally validated
Does not cover polymer science or soft matter physics in full depth (liquid crystals, colloids, biological matter)
Strongly correlated systems (heavy fermions, Mott insulators) require beyond-mean-field methods — no general solution
Non-equilibrium condensed matter (driven systems, Floquet phases) is an active frontier
Room-temperature ambient-pressure superconductivity has not been reliably achieved
key_references: Kittel, C. Introduction to Solid State Physics
Altland & Simons. Condensed Matter Field Theory
Laughlin, R.B. (1983). Quantized Hall Conductance (Nobel lecture)
Anderson, P.W. Basic Notions of Condensed Matter Physics
Wen, X.G. Quantum Field Theory of Many-Body Systems
canonical_truths: Room-temperature ambient-pressure superconductivity has not been reliably achieved despite decades of effort
Topological insulators conduct electricity on their surface but are insulating in the bulk — protected by time-reversal symmetry
Semiconductor band gaps (Si: 1.12 eV, GaAs: 1.43 eV) enable all modern electronics and photovoltaics
Quantum Hall effect: σ_xy = ne²/h (where n is integer) — exact quantization independent of material details
BCS critical temperature: T_c ~ 1-30 K for conventional superconductors; unconventional cuprates reach ~160 K at high pressure
Graphene (2010 Nobel) has a Dirac cone band structure — electrons behave as massless Dirac fermions
Magnetic order (ferro-, antiferro-, ferrimagnetism) arises from exchange interactions between electron spins
